author: @electrolysisresearch8013
text: You need to stay out of my head and stop stealing my ideas lol. I'm working on printing one like this based off data i collected with a prototype, made from 3 5 gallon buckets, the thing was poorly balanced using a bicycle wheel as its bearing, it was too heavy and needed a second bearing yet was still producing over 300 watts. There is an interesting trick with geometry where the updraft meets the downdraft on the turbine blade that I learned. But I actually have geometry with very easy math on how to shape your blades. I learned that doing a spiral is wrong if you have the blades shaped correctly, it's really difficult to explain but with the correct geometry you want your blades to be straight not twisted, the trick is to make it taper wider in the middle. I did a extremely high tech test using a house fan and smoke, to see it make a vortex pattern when air from the top of the blade collide with the air from the bottom. So I added a divider and it increased the effect further.

author: @Sailorman6996
text:
The Ugrinsky original model will be more efficient than the spiral version of the same. As long as you don't add something to stop air slip along the vertical axis. In your case when you stack them as you did on your desk - any radial wind will both turn the turbine and slip upwards. A flat disk would reduce the slip effect. But it looks cool, and even better when it spins.

If Ugrinsky is more efficient than Savonius - I don't know. But Savonius might be ten time easier to make. A tin, or a bucket with a lid or even a barrel is very easy to use as material for a Savonius.
Good luck and have fun :)

author: @kadmow
text:
NB. I too love VAWT - with interest in variable pitch - for near continuous potential power at every point of the wind.. (think of each blade like a high performance sailboat sailing around the wind with every rotation..  Most  VAWT turbines (fixed pitch - even aero styles like  Darreius) only generates thrust on a very limited portion of the wind circle.

 -  maybe a feature - of the spiral style turbines is if one attempts to "over generate" - ie. run it too slow with "too much generator" - the wind loses drive and just spirals out the top of the "turbine -  the baffles will tend to disturb this and may increase drive - OR drag under stalled conditions..  The mode of operation is slightly different to that of a pelton wheel / anemometer (the most simple VAWT).  

With Drag Devices - "surface imperfections" on the windward side 9high pressure) increase drive, but on the lee side may tend to increase drag, though "specifically engineered "imperfections" would win over random print lines...   it is all in low cost fun.......

author: @PeterWolfe2012
text:
Now that's interesting.  And like so many interesting things it has a separate layer of "What else can we do with it?".  

Can you modify the printer file so that the "tunnel" section has a shorter chord, (experimenting first with maintaining the same chord relationship between the chord and the other parts and maybe layer changing keeping the current width of the "mouth" and "tunnel" while using the shorter chord) and arranging three, or five, or eight of these "mouth and tunnel" sections spaced evenly around a hollow center section to take advantage of the smaller sections smaller mass acting on effectively "longer" levers to see what happens when the the unique part of the design has less mass and more leverage. 

 If that small scale, (limited to the width of the 3-D printer), seems promising maybe a larger version can be printed in sections that can be combined to form the drag components attached to longer lever arms. 

 I'm sure I've seen straight and spiraled "buckets" attached to longer arms around a hollow center before.  I know I've thought of it long enough and often enough to believe that it MUST be pretty common.  But I'd like to know if this novel design would add enough efficiency to that basic layout to worthwhile.

author: @PaulLemars01
text:
Robert, this is an interesting project that a 3D printer company would really like to get involved with. You certainly have the subscriber base for them to be interested in you. I would suggest you reach out to a company named Flsun. They specialize in what are known as delta printers. They might lend you or give you their V400 printer which is about four times as fast as your Elegoo printer as well as the print volume being about 50% bigger. Don't get me wrong, The Elegoo is a nice entry level printer, but if you want something that can output stuff quickly it would behoove you to look at a delta printer. Also I would suggest you switch to PETG filament it's a more robust filament and it's about the same price as PLA.

Actually ask for two V400s. Better to be hung for a sheep than a lamb.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: A good way to make these fast and cheap is to print a split mold.  So you then pour in the resin and wait to cure say 1 hour.  Then split and remove the mold and place near the top of the hardened first piece. You pour in some more resin and let it cure. And simply repeat as the part grows with each pour of resin. Far quicker and cheaper to make. Also no gluing needed. And stronger than a printed version.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice suggestion mate - print one as the mold blank?

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering You actually print the mold that you use for the model. Just remember to spray release agent in the mold before you pour in the resin every time. If you want to do it super fast, print 2 molds and while one is setting  you are preparing and pouring the second mold. It is amazing how fast they come out then.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering You realise you can get a turn around time per mold is around 3 minutes? Let me explain. You print out 20 molds. You then prepare a mold for pouring with release agent and you mix resin and pour it in all in 3 minutes.  You then go to the next mold and do the same. Then the next mold. By an hour later you are back to the first mold and you remove the resin  and start new run. If in your vawt you use 5 high pieces it will take 15 minutes to do a full 5 stages of poured resin to make 1 full length  of the vawt. Basically this is a mini mass production run using printed molds.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering BTW you print molds using a rubber composit compound not hard plastic. See the various types of 3D materials available.

author: @gavinlynas2833
text: May I suggest a different type of wind power generator. A long shaft going directly up into the air with a paddle like flatness at the end. The idea is that the wind will blow on this creating enormous leaverage at the bottom, and although not moving very far, can be geared up in some way to spin a generator very fast. Wind is intermittent so when it lets up the shaft will partially or fully return to its original upright position. The shaft would bend partially creating a spring back effect if something like a bar to bend against was placed around the middle of the shaft for it to bend over. This negates the need to have a spring/rubber band/counterweight to bring it back into position and therefore detract from the overall would be power generated. Movement may and probably will be quite subtle with perhaps the wind not letting up much, but even with slight movements the leaverage would be monumental at the the bottom, and that small movement can be upscaled into a lot provided there is sufficient power to enforce it which there will be. The benefit of all this is that you can go much higher where the wind is and there is perhaps way more bang for your buck as far a power output is conserned. All the fiddly complicated bits are at ground level also rather than spinning up in the air. Should be much easier to erect as well.

author: @SchwaAlien
text: Bed slinger type printers are nice and cheap for the build volume but there’s very little you can do about the speed of prints because of the bed mass having to move which limits quality at higher speeds due to inertia, that’s the advantage of the delta and core xy type printers, they are able to print much faster (given appropriate electronics) due to only the print head moving, changing directions rapidly.  I have a slow bed slinger (cheapest dual extruder I could find) and a delta which was limited by the 8-bit micro controller to 50mm/s with Octoprint and 60mm/s from SD card but I upgraded it to Klipper firmware so now the Raspberry Pi handles the delta computation and the 8bit controller only has to handle motion and sensors which lets it print as fast as the nozzle can extrude filament, around 100mm/s, gotta upgrade the hot end for more flow and then I could reach higher speeds but adequate cooling is also important and the Voron designs where extra fixed cooling at print nozzle height seem to have some big advantage over deltas when it comes to getting super fast.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text:
I like it!  Not sure if you can make a mold to mass produce?
Secondly, when you find the design you like best if not this one, here is a nice idea for improving it!
Let me first discuss an issue then solve it.
Issue is if you connect a bunch of them on a shaft, if one turbine goes to fast it can be slowed due to the other fans going slower.  HOWEVER, solution, similar to gearing for auto wheels on a turn, if the shaft only turns if blade goes 1.  In one direction, 2.  Or blade goes faster than current shaft speed.  Meaning make the blade so it only turns one direction and will release of the blade goes faster as to not slow the shaft down.  
In other words, the blades can only effect the speed of the shaft IF and only if it's going as fast or faster than the shaft like a check valve goes only one way to prevent one slower blade from reducing the faster spinning blade or blades.  This will maximize shaft speed and you can hook a motor/generator on both ends of the shaft  for redundancy.  If one motor fails, you have a backup in place.  If you want to take it a step further, you can even do the same with the shaft so it both sides of the shaft can hook together with each motor optimized for certain rpms corresponding to winds speeds optimizing power for higher and lower rotations, this higher and lower rpms.  But for now focus on the blades only effecting the one shaft if they can add to the shaft and individual blade max rpm.  If blade is going slower it won't slow down the shaft 
GL!

replies.author: @SchwaAlien
replies.text: PLA is nice and easy to print with, doesn’t usually offgas nasty fumes, but it also doesn’t hold up in hot temperatures (boiling water will easily deform it) and acts more brittle than other plastics, and it shrinks if tempered, so can cause trouble depending on the situation.  I also print with PETG when I need a more durable print, it is much more flexible, more heat resistant (prints at higher temperatures but cheap Bowden printers can still handle) and also doesn’t offgas anything too nasty.  I used it for a slow-close toilet seat hinge that broke and a wallet / money clip that I’ve used since 2018, so it tests out satisfactory in real world durability.  I bought a lot of ABS when I first got my printer but returned it before ever printing with it because I didn’t realize it would offgas significantly when printing, which makes sense, just need appropriate ventilation which would have meant printing in the only bathroom - not exactly the best situation.

replies.author: @gavinatkin9651
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering unfortunately PLA won't stand up to heat outside where PETG would most probably be better. I have been pondering on a system that could house the whole turbine, protecting it from the elements. It should perhaps have an aerofoil on the air inlet which scoops a large amount of wind, uses a venturi effect to boost the wind between the aerofoil and turbine to increase the flow and use a rudder to face it into the wind / divert it if too strong. Also, the housing could block the drag side of the turbine maximising flow onto the "scoop" side of the aerofoil. I am 57 and love your idea generating work! I will never go into retirement with these projects.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: @gavinatkin9651 If using drag VAWTs in a wind wall I think making alternate ones counter rotation then blocking the  combined drag side with a triangle ( point facing out ) should serve to not just lessen the  -ve torque of the drag side but also divert that oncoming air to the +ve scoop side. Probably not ultimately as efficient as your idea but it's advantages are it's very cheap and simple to implement, doesn't add much more space taken up by the wall itself and adds no more moving parts.

replies.author: @tonywilson4713
replies.text:
VAWTS are about to become huge.
Its almost impossible to put a large HAWTs on top of a tall buildings like you get in the CBDs of many cities because of the loads on the roof. But the tops of those building are up out of the ground clutter where the wind is almost constant 24/7. There's too much emphasis been put on performance instead of practicality.

The other place these will take off when enough people work it out are in places where people already have solar and they are near to large water bodies that create a sea breeze. Most sea breezes start in the late afternoon after the ground has spent the day heating up. So at the time solar power is diminishing while at the same time wind is picking up and will usually last until the morning when the sun comes out. So a VAWT that's built to output power like a solar panel plugs straight into the existing solar inverter.There are some starting to come out but they are expensive.
